title: Unit 03 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Budgeting, Saving, and Money Management.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a budget?

choices.text: To plan how money will be earned, spent, and saved
choices.feedback: Correct. A budget helps organize income, expenses, and saving decisions.

choices.text: To remove all financial choices
choices.feedback: A budget supports decisions, but it does not remove the need to make choices.

choices.text: To guarantee that every purchase is a good idea
choices.feedback: A budget helps evaluate purchases, but it does not guarantee every decision is wise.

choices.text: To replace all saving goals
choices.feedback: Saving goals are part of budgeting, not something a budget replaces.

questions.prompt: An emergency fund is money saved for unexpected expenses.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: An emergency fund is specifically meant for unexpected costs.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Emergency funds help protect financial stability when unplanned costs happen.

questions.prompt: Which account is usually the better fit for money a person plans to spend regularly?

choices.text: Savings account only
choices.feedback: Savings accounts are often used for money set aside for later use.

choices.text: Checking account
choices.feedback: Correct. Checking accounts are commonly used for regular transactions and spending.

choices.text: Emergency fund only
choices.feedback: An emergency fund is a purpose for saved money, not the main account type for regular spending.

choices.text: Any account that ignores budget limits
choices.feedback: Strong account choices should support, not ignore, money management.

questions.prompt: A student has $90 in weekly income and plans to spend $25 on transportation, $20 on food, and $15 on savings. How much money remains?

choices.feedback: Check the total expenses first and then subtract from the income.
This total is too high after subtracting the planned categories from the income.
Correct. The planned total is $60, and $90 minus $60 leaves $30.
The remaining amount is lower once all planned categories are included.

questions.prompt: Impulse buying can make it harder to stay on budget.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Unplanned purchases can reduce money available for needs, savings, or other priorities.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Impulse buying can interfere with a financial plan and create budget problems.

questions.prompt: Why is saving money regularly considered a strong financial strategy?

choices.text: Because it can support goals and financial security over time
choices.feedback: This is true, but the correct answer in this set is placed elsewhere.

choices.text: Because it guarantees that no unexpected expenses will ever happen
choices.feedback: Saving helps prepare for unexpected costs, but it does not prevent them.

choices.text: Because it removes the need to think about spending choices
choices.feedback: Saving and spending decisions still require comparison and planning.

choices.text: Because it can support goals and financial security over time
choices.feedback: Correct. Regular saving supports future plans and financial stability.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains the difference between checking and savings accounts?

choices.text: Checking is usually for regular spending, while savings is usually for money set aside for later use
choices.feedback: This is correct reasoning, but the correct answer in this set is placed elsewhere.

choices.text: Checking and savings always serve the exact same purpose
choices.feedback: These account types often support different financial purposes.

choices.text: Checking is usually for regular spending, while savings is usually for money set aside for later use
choices.feedback: Correct. Checking supports access and transactions, while savings supports future use.

choices.text: Savings is only used for daily purchases
choices.feedback: Savings is more commonly connected to money set aside rather than frequent daily use.

questions.prompt: A student has $40 left after planned expenses and is considering a $28 unplanned purchase. What is the strongest financial concern?

choices.text: The purchase leaves only $12 for the rest of the week
choices.feedback: This is the correct concern, but the correct answer in this set is placed elsewhere.

choices.text: The purchase automatically improves saving progress
choices.feedback: An unplanned purchase usually reduces available money instead of improving saving progress.

choices.text: The purchase means budgeting is no longer necessary
choices.feedback: Budgeting is still necessary, especially when evaluating an unplanned expense.

choices.text: The purchase leaves only $12 for the rest of the week
choices.feedback: Correct. The main concern is that little money remains after the unplanned spending choice.
